text:
MEDICAL ONCOLOGY -- FOLLOW-UP VISIT
Patient: Raymond Kowalski | DOB on file | MRN on file
Attending: [Attending Oncologist]
Visit Type: Established Patient -- Treatment Response Review

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT
Mr. Kowalski is a 58-year-old Polish-American male with Stage IV sigmoid colon adenocarcinoma (KRAS G12D mutant, pMMR/MSS) with hepatic and pulmonary metastases, presenting for follow-up approximately 14 months from initial diagnosis and 4 months into second-line FOLFIRI plus ramucirumab therapy, for treatment response review and ongoing toxicity monitoring.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS
Mr. Kowalski was originally diagnosed with Stage IV (M1a) sigmoid colon adenocarcinoma roughly 14 months ago, presenting with synchronous bilobar liver metastases. He completed 12 cycles of first-line modified FOLFOX-6 plus bevacizumab, achieving a confirmed partial response (best response) before transitioning to maintenance LV5FU2 plus bevacizumab per OPTIMOX-based strategy. Unfortunately, at approximately 10 months from diagnosis, surveillance imaging demonstrated clear hepatic progression (Segment VI and IVb lesions growing) and, more significantly, new bilateral pulmonary nodules consistent with metastatic spread -- representing progression on maintenance therapy. He was transitioned to second-line FOLFIRI plus ramucirumab (Cyramza) at that time, consistent with the RAISE trial data supporting OS benefit of ramucirumab over bevacizumab continuation in this setting.

He has now completed approximately 8 cycles of FOLFIRI/ramucirumab over the past 4 months. Today we are reviewing his most recent cross-sectional imaging, which was obtained 8 weeks ago and reviewed in detail below. In brief, response imaging demonstrates a partial response by my assessment -- the hepatic lesions (Segment VI down from 2.1 to 1.8 cm, Segment IVb from 1.0 to 0.8 cm) have clearly shrunk, and the pulmonary nodules are either stable or minimally reduced in size. The sigmoid primary tumor remains largely stable around 2.7 cm. There is no new metastatic disease. CEA has decreased from 67.3 ng/mL at the point of progression to 44.1 ng/mL currently, and CA 19-9 has declined from 187 to 134 U/mL, both consistent with meaningful treatment response.

From a symptom standpoint, Mr. Kowalski reports he is doing reasonably well. He continues to have mild loose stools approximately 2-3 episodes per day, well-managed with intermittent loperamide. He denies frank watery diarrhea at this point; this is a notable improvement from the Grade 2 diarrhea experienced early in this regimen. He reports mild fatigue, most prominent in the 3-4 days following each infusion, after which he recovers adequately. His residual peripheral neuropathy from prior oxaliplatin is stable -- mild tingling in the fingertips bilaterally, not worsening on irinotecan. Blood pressure has been well-controlled at home since starting amlodipine; he is monitoring twice daily and averaging systolics around 132-138 per his log. He has not experienced any new bleeding, epistaxis, or hemoptysis. No new neurologic symptoms. No fever or neutropenic symptoms.

He remains retired and is living at home with his wife. He has been able to resume some light activities including short walks in the neighborhood and occasional visits with his grandchildren. He played golf once last month (modified, using a cart). He is in reasonable spirits and remains motivated to continue treatment.

---

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS
Constitutional: Mild fatigue (Grade 1), post-infusion pattern, improving between cycles. No significant weight loss (down approximately 2 lbs from last visit). No fevers or night sweats.
GI: Mild loose stools 2-3x/day, well-managed with loperamide prn. No nausea or vomiting currently (was Grade 1 early in this regimen). No melena, hematochezia, change in stool caliber. No abdominal pain or cramping at rest; mild infusion-day cramping managed with atropine per protocol.
Cardiovascular: No chest pain, palpitations, or shortness of breath. Blood pressure controlled per home log. No lower extremity edema.
Neurologic: Mild bilateral distal fingertip tingling, stable from prior visits (residual oxaliplatin neuropathy, Grade 1). No new sensory or motor deficits.
Dermatologic: Mild alopecia (continued from irinotecan, Grade 1). No significant hand-foot syndrome at this time.
Pulmonary: No dyspnea, cough, or hemoptysis. No pleuritic chest pain.
MSK: No new bone pain.
Other systems: Negative unless noted above.

---

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION
Vital signs: BP 136/84 mmHg (left arm, sitting), HR 74 bpm (regular), RR 14, Temp 98.3 F, SpO2 98% on room air, Weight 172 lbs (BMI 27.1).
General: Well-appearing male, in no acute distress. Mild temporal muscle wasting noted. Mood is engaged and appropriate.
Head/Eyes: Anicteric sclerae. No conjunctival pallor.
Neck: No lymphadenopathy. Thyroid non-palpable.
Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm. No murmurs. No S3 or S4. No peripheral edema.
Pulmonary: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. No wheeze, rhonchi, or crackles.
Abdomen: Soft, non-distended. Mild tenderness to deep palpation in left lower quadrant (overlying sigmoid region), no guarding or rebound. Liver edge palpable 2 cm below the right costal margin, smooth, non-tender. No splenomegaly. Port site right chest wall -- clean, no tenderness, no erythema or induration.
Extremities: No clubbing, cyanosis, or edema. Mild alopecia noted on scalp.
Neurologic: Alert and oriented x3. Cranial nerves II-XII grossly intact. Gait normal. Vibratory sense mildly reduced bilateral great toes (unchanged from prior). Pinprick sensation intact in all extremities. Fine motor intact -- patient buttons his shirt without difficulty today (improved from earlier in course).

---

LABORATORY RESULTS
CBC: WBC 5.1 x10^9/L, Hgb 10.4 g/dL (Grade 1 anemia, chronic/chemotherapy-related, stable), MCV 88, Plt 182 x10^9/L, ANC 2.0 x10^9/L.
Comprehensive metabolic panel: Na 138, K 4.1, CO2 24, BUN 14, Creatinine 0.9 mg/dL, eGFR >60. Glucose 108 (diet-controlled DM, acceptable). LFTs: AST 58, ALT 64 (mildly elevated, consistent with hepatic metastatic disease, similar to prior), Alk Phos 112, Total bili 0.8, Albumin 3.8.
Urine protein:creatinine ratio: 0.19 (stable, well below the 2.0 threshold for ramucirumab hold -- no clinically significant proteinuria).
Coagulation: INR 1.1.
Tumor markers: CEA 44.1 ng/mL (down from 67.3 at time of progression, down from 148.2 at original diagnosis). CA 19-9: 134 U/mL (down from 187 at progression).

---

IMAGING RESULTS
CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with IV contrast (reviewed, obtained approximately 8 weeks ago):
Sigmoid primary: Stable at approximately 2.7 x 2.3 cm, no bowel obstruction.
Liver: Segment VI lesion decreased to 1.8 x 1.5 cm (from 2.1 x 1.8 cm at progression); Segment IVb decreased to 0.8 x 0.7 cm (from 1.0 x 0.9 cm); Segment II stable near-complete response at 0.5 x 0.4 cm.
Lungs: Right lower lobe nodule 0.7 x 0.6 cm (minimally decreased); left upper lobe nodule 0.5 x 0.5 cm (stable); right middle lobe nodule 0.4 x 0.3 cm (stable to slightly decreased). No new pulmonary nodules identified.
No new sites of metastatic disease. No ascites. No lymphadenopathy.
Overall: Favorable partial response on FOLFIRI/ramucirumab. Hepatic disease clearly responding. Pulmonary nodules stable to minimally improved.

---

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN

1. Stage IV sigmoid colon adenocarcinoma (KRAS G12D mutant, pMMR/MSS) with hepatic and pulmonary metastases -- partial response on second-line FOLFIRI plus ramucirumab.

Mr. Kowalski is doing well on FOLFIRI/ramucirumab at 4 months in. He is achieving a meaningful partial response by both imaging and tumor marker trends, which is encouraging given that second-line therapy in this KRAS-mutant MSS CRC context is not expected to be curative. The hepatic lesions are clearly responding and the lung nodules are stable. I am pleased with where we are. We will continue FOLFIRI plus ramucirumab. Repeat CT chest/abdomen/pelvis in approximately 8 weeks for ongoing response assessment.

We briefly revisited the question of surgical consolidation or ablative therapy for the liver. Given the continued presence of bilateral hepatic disease and now established lung metastases, I do not think hepatic resection is appropriate to pursue at this juncture -- this is now M1b disease and hepatectomy would not be curative. Hepatobiliary surgery concurs per prior discussions. If continued excellent hepatic response occurs with future near-CR, we may revisit ablative approaches (RFA) for dominant lesions, but this is not a current priority.

Given that this patient has KRAS G12D-mutant disease, I again reviewed whether any KRAS G12D-directed approaches might be applicable. MRTX1133 and other non-covalent KRAS G12D inhibitors remain in early phase trials. I will flag for clinical trial enrollment when/if we reach progression on FOLFIRI/ramucirumab, as eligibility criteria may be met at that point.

2. Chemotherapy toxicities:
   a. Irinotecan-related diarrhea: Currently Grade 1, improved from Grade 2 earlier in course. Patient managing well with loperamide as needed. Continue current management. UGT1A1 *1/*28 heterozygous -- tolerating 180 mg/m2 without dose reduction required at this time.
   b. Hypertension (ramucirumab-related): BP 136/84 today on lisinopril 20 mg + amlodipine 5 mg. Grade 1, well-controlled. No medication changes needed. Continue dual antihypertensive regimen. Urine protein:creatinine ratio 0.19, acceptable.
   c. Residual oxaliplatin peripheral neuropathy: Grade 1, stable, mild fingertip tingling. Not worsening on irinotecan, as expected. Continue monitoring. Physical therapy exercises ongoing per neurology recommendations.
   d. Anemia: Hgb 10.4, Grade 1, stable trend. No transfusion required at this time. Iron studies previously reviewed (iron-replete). Continue monitoring.
   e. Alopecia: Grade 1. Patient aware and accepting.

3. Comorbidities:
   a. Hypertension: Managed as above.
   b. Hyperlipidemia: Continue atorvastatin 40 mg. LFTs mildly elevated but stable -- statin-related contribution unlikely given stable trend consistent with hepatic metastases. No change.
   c. Type 2 diabetes mellitus (diet-controlled): Glucose 108 today, HbA1c not checked today but will recheck at next visit. Continue dietary management.

4. Quality of life and psychosocial:
Patient is maintaining good functional status (ECOG 1). Continues to live at home, engage in light activities, and has time with family including grandchildren. Psychosocially, he and his wife appear well-adjusted to the ongoing treatment course. He was offered a palliative care referral previously and deferred, but I mentioned today that the palliative care team can help with symptom management even while continuing active treatment. He is open to this and I will re-extend the referral. Social work has not yet been engaged. I will re-offer.

5. Advanced care planning: Patient has completed advance directive with wife as healthcare proxy. We briefly reinforced this today. He understands the goals of current therapy are disease control and prolongation of survival rather than cure. He remains committed to continued active treatment with maintenance of quality of life as his primary personal goal.

MEDICATIONS (current):
- Ramucirumab (Cyramza) 8 mg/kg IV q14 days
- Irinotecan 180 mg/m2 IV q14 days
- Leucovorin 400 mg/m2 IV q14 days
- 5-Fluorouracil 400 mg/m2 IV bolus + 2400 mg/m2 IV over 46 hours q14 days
- Lisinopril 20 mg PO daily
- Amlodipine 5 mg PO daily
- Atorvastatin 40 mg PO qhs
- Ondansetron 8 mg PO q8h PRN nausea (post-infusion days)
- Loperamide 4 mg PO initially, then 2 mg after each loose stool PRN (max 16 mg/day)
- Atropine 0.25 mg IV (given at infusion center for irinotecan cholinergic symptoms, PRN)

ALLERGIES: NKDA

RETURN: Next infusion cycle in 2 weeks per schedule. Next office visit with repeat imaging review in approximately 8 weeks.

Time spent: 45 minutes, >50% counseling and coordination of care.

Electronically signed by: [Attending Oncologist, MD], Medical Oncology
